channel.title: Jax Morning Brief
channel.description: Your daily rundown of national headlines, Jacksonville and Northeast Florida local news, home lending updates, and the latest in AI. Produced by Akilesh.
channel.itunes:author: Akilesh

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — US Seizes Iranian Ship, Ceasefire Expiring Wednesday, Warsh Faces Senate Tomorrow
item.description: The US Navy seized an Iranian cargo ship Saturday as the two-week ceasefire expires Wednesday. Kevin Warsh faces the Senate Banking Committee tomorrow. Mortgage rates hit a five-week low at 6.3 percent.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning, and welcome back from the weekend. It's Monday, April 20th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: The United States Navy fired on and seized an Iranian cargo ship over the weekend, and Tehran is now threatening retaliation. The two-week ceasefire expires Wednesday — with no deal in sight and fresh fighting words on both sides.

ANDREW: And Kevin Warsh sits before the Senate Banking Committee tomorrow morning for his confirmation hearing to become the next Federal Reserve chair. It is the most consequential monetary policy hearing in nearly a decade, and the outcome could move markets before the week is out.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets first. The S and P 500 closed Friday at roughly 7,126, up about one point two percent. The Dow finished near 49,450, up one point eight percent. The Nasdaq closed at around 24,470, up one and a half percent. It was a broadly positive week for equities, extending a two-week rally built on Middle East de-escalation hopes — though those hopes got complicated over the weekend. The ten-year Treasury yield settled Friday at four point two six percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is at six point three percent according to Freddie Mac's latest weekly survey — a five-week low for borrowers.

ANDREW: Before we get into the beats, a quick weekend at a glance. The single biggest development was Saturday's seizure of an Iranian cargo ship by the U.S. Navy in the Gulf of Oman. By Sunday, Iran's military had formally threatened retaliation, and Vice President JD Vance was on his way to Pakistan for what the White House is billing as a second round of direct talks — though Tehran has publicly said it has no plan to send anyone to the table.

ANDREW: Let's start with what drove all of it. The two-week ceasefire between the United States and Iran — brokered by Pakistan and signed on April 8th — was built on a straightforward bargain: the U.S. would pause strikes on Iranian infrastructure, and Iran would keep the Strait of Hormuz open to commercial traffic. That bargain has been eroding since almost the first day. The U.S. maintained its naval blockade of Iranian ports. Iran periodically re-closed the Strait. And then Saturday happened.

ANDREW: The U.S. Navy intercepted an Iranian-flagged cargo ship called the Touska in the Gulf of Oman. According to President Trump, the crew was given fair warning to stand down, refused, and the Navy fired on the vessel's engine room to stop it. The Touska is a sanctioned Iranian vessel. Iran immediately called the boarding an act of piracy and a ceasefire violation. By Sunday morning, the Iranian military had issued a formal warning of retaliation.

JENNY: So where does that leave the Wednesday deadline? Is there any real diplomatic path here?

ANDREW: That's the core question going into this week. The White House confirmed Sunday that Vice President Vance, along with special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, are heading back to Islamabad for a second round of talks. This is the same delegation that spent twenty-one hours in Pakistan two weeks ago and came home without a deal. The difference now is that Iran's Foreign Ministry has publicly said there is no plan for new talks. So the U.S. team is flying toward a table that the other side hasn't agreed to sit at.

JENNY: And what happens if Wednesday arrives with no extension?

ANDREW: The scenario the markets are pricing in — but not yet fully accounting for — is a resumption of U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iranian infrastructure. That means oil. The Strait of Hormuz handles roughly one-fifth of the world's crude. A full breakdown, with Iran keeping the Strait closed and hostilities resuming, almost certainly pushes oil back toward and possibly above a hundred dollars a barrel. That feeds directly into inflation, which puts the Fed in a bind. And there's another clock ticking: the War Powers Act sixty-day deadline hits around May 1st, which would require Trump to get congressional authorization to continue military operations. No administration has ever actually complied with that, but it adds legal and political pressure at an already volatile moment.

ANDREW: On a separate note, also from the weekend — President Trump signed an executive order Friday directing the FDA to accelerate its review of psychedelic therapies that have already received breakthrough drug designation. Ibogaine, which has shown real promise for treating opioid addiction and PTSD in veterans, is the most prominent example. The order tells the Department of Health and Human Services to fast-track research approvals and expand patient access. Veterans advocacy groups from both parties have been pushing for exactly this kind of action, which gives it unusual bipartisan footing — but it will still face the FDA's existing approval infrastructure, and that process does not move quickly.

JENNY: Andrew, before we get to home lending — the Warsh hearing tomorrow feels like it touches everything right now. Rates, Fed independence, inflation. What should our listeners actually be watching for?

ANDREW: Great framing. Kevin Warsh testifies at ten a.m. Eastern before the Senate Banking Committee. He's Trump's pick to replace Jerome Powell, whose term expires May 15th. This is not a low-stakes hearing.

ANDREW: The financial disclosure piece has drawn real attention. Warsh has reported assets valued between a hundred thirty-one million and two hundred nine million dollars — substantially more wealth than any previous Fed chair nominee, according to reporting from The Hill and Fortune. That includes stakes in SpaceX and in Polymarket, the prediction market platform. Senators will have pointed questions about both. The Fed chair's forward guidance moves global markets. If you have a stake in prediction markets, there are obvious conflict-of-interest questions that the committee is going to press.

JENNY: And Senator Tillis is still a complication.

ANDREW: He is. Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina has made clear he will not vote for any Fed chair nominee until the Justice Department drops its investigation into Powell and the Fed's multibillion-dollar headquarters renovation project. Tillis is a senior Republican on that committee. If he holds, Warsh could face a very thin margin — or could stall entirely. Watch whether Tillis gives any signal tomorrow that he is softening. That's the tell on whether Warsh's path to confirmation is clear.

JENNY: And Powell has been pretty unambiguous that he's not going anywhere voluntarily.

ANDREW: Very much so. Powell has said publicly he intends to serve out his term, and legal consensus is that the president cannot fire a Fed governor except for cause — which is a high and undefined standard. Trump has threatened on Fox Business to force Powell out if he does not leave when his term expires. That threat has rattled markets before. Tomorrow's hearing should clarify whether Warsh is willing to distance himself from that kind of pressure or whether he'll be seen as a political instrument. How he answers questions on Fed independence may matter as much as anything else he says.

JENNY: All right, let me pick this up from the rate side. Are mortgage rates feeling any of this volatility?

ANDREW: Favorably, for now. Freddie Mac's latest weekly average has the thirty-year fixed at six point three percent, which is the lowest in about five weeks. What's happening is that geopolitical uncertainty has been pushing money into Treasuries — investors treat them as a safe haven — which drives Treasury prices up and yields down. Mortgage rates tend to track the ten-year yield closely, and that yield has dropped from around four point six percent a month ago to four point two six percent as of Friday.

JENNY: So buyers who have been waiting are actually catching a window here?

ANDREW: They are, with a clear caveat. If the Iran ceasefire collapses Wednesday and oil spikes, you get an inflationary shock — and the Fed cannot cut rates into an inflationary shock. Rates could climb back quickly. For buyers in the market right now, the practical calculus this week is: if your loan is ready, locking before Wednesday's deadline might be worth the certainty. The MBA's mortgage application data comes out later this week, and that will tell us whether buyers are actually acting on the lower rates or still sitting on the sidelines.

ANDREW: One more wrinkle directly tied to tomorrow. If Warsh signals in his hearing that he would favor easier monetary policy — which some analysts expect — yields could fall further, which helps rates. If the hearing turns contentious, or if Tillis creates a visible standoff, you could see the opposite. Watch the ten-year yield in real time after the hearing opens. It is a live indicator of how the bond market is reading the testimony.

JENNY: Smart thing to watch. Okay, let me move to AI and technology — and the story of this week is about what agentic AI looks like when it actually goes to work.

ANDREW: What do you mean by that?

JENNY: EY — one of the Big Four accounting firms — has done something that genuinely stands apart from all the AI pilot programs we keep hearing about. They have deployed a multi-agent AI framework across their entire global assurance workforce. Not a test. Not a cohort. The whole workforce: a hundred thirty thousand auditing professionals, conducting a hundred sixty thousand audits in more than a hundred fifty countries. The system is built on Microsoft Azure and embedded directly into EY Canvas, their core audit platform.

ANDREW: And what are the agents actually doing?

JENNY: They're embedded in all phases of the audit workflow — sampling, documentation review, anomaly flagging, risk assessment — under human review at each stage. EY's framing is that auditors are being augmented, not replaced, and they have built a global retraining program to upskill the whole workforce. But when autonomous agents are embedded in the process for a hundred sixty thousand audits, the distinction between assistance and decision-making starts to compress. The accounting profession's regulators — the bodies that set audit standards — have not kept pace with how fast this technology has moved, and that gap is the risk that practitioners and clients will be watching.

JENNY: To put EY in context: OpenAI crossed twenty-five billion dollars in annualized revenue this month, according to CNBC, and Anthropic is approaching nineteen billion. Both companies are growing not primarily on consumer subscriptions now — they're growing on enterprise workforce deployments exactly like this one. The business model has shifted from selling AI access to embedding AI agents in how work actually gets done. EY's deployment is the most visible proof point yet that the shift is real and at scale.

ANDREW: So the question is: who sets the rules for those agents in a regulated profession?

JENNY: Exactly. Audit opinions carry legal weight. Financial statements rely on them. If an agent-assisted audit misses something material, the liability framework — which was written for human auditors — does not map cleanly onto an AI-assisted process. That is an open question that regulators, law firms, and the firms themselves are going to be wrestling with for the next several years.

JENNY: Let's head to Jacksonville. Weather-wise, it's a great start to the week — a high near 75 degrees, sunny skies, with a breezy northeast wind around 15 miles an hour. One flag if you're heading to the beach: there is a rip current statement in effect through early Tuesday morning, so use caution in the surf.

JENNY: The big local development story is the University of Florida's downtown Jacksonville campus taking a major step forward. UF has selected Edgemoor Infrastructure and Real Estate as the lead developer for the initial phase of its Jacksonville graduate campus — a first building valued at two hundred eighty-seven and a half million dollars. Edgemoor is bringing in a team that includes Clark Construction and local contractor Auld and White Constructors, so there's a Jacksonville presence in the build from the start.

ANDREW: This has been in the works for a while. What does the timeline look like?

JENNY: UF says it will open initial programs in existing downtown facilities this year, while new construction gets underway. The longer vision is a full research and graduate campus that the city sees as a talent anchor — the kind of institution that produces the workforce pipeline companies like Winn-Dixie are here to draw from. And speaking of Winn-Dixie: the twelve-million-dollar incentive package that cleared the Mayor's Budget Review Committee last week is set for formal introduction to City Council on April 28th. In exchange for the incentives — a combination of a recapture grant and a headquarters retention grant — Winn-Dixie commits to a sixty-five-million-dollar investment, retaining five hundred existing jobs and creating two hundred new positions at an average salary above a hundred thousand dollars. The MBRC vote was unanimous, and the April 28th introduction will be the first full Council review.

ANDREW: Any organized opposition at this point?

JENNY: Nothing public yet, but the Council introduction will surface scrutiny around the incentive-per-job math and the 2031 milestone structure. The deal is performance-based, which gives it defensibility, but these packages always draw questions in a public hearing. We will follow it.

JENNY: And the JEA situation continues to simmer. The four active investigations — State Attorney Melissa Nelson's subpoena, the City Council Special Investigatory Committee, and two others — are all running in parallel. No new indictments or subpoenas emerged this weekend. The situation between CEO Vickie Cavey, Council President Kevin Carrico, and the Ballard Partners lobbying connection remains unresolved. This one will be with us for a while.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week — and it's the most consequential single moment we have on the calendar. Wednesday, April 22nd is the expiration of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire. The specific thing to watch for is whether Iran's Foreign Ministry changes its current posture — which, as of Sunday, was a flat "no plan" for new talks — before Tuesday evening. If Tehran confirms attendance at the Pakistan talks before Wednesday, you will likely see an extension announced, oil will fall, and equities will rally. If Tuesday night comes and goes with no Iranian signal, you are looking at a probable breakdown at midnight Wednesday, with the risk of resumed strikes and an oil shock that reshapes the economic picture for the rest of the second quarter. Watch that Foreign Ministry statement. That is your leading indicator.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Monday. Have a great start to the week.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Lebanon Ceasefire Tested, Markets Hit Records, Builders Lose Confidence
item.description: A fragile Israel-Lebanon ceasefire takes effect amid reported violations as forty countries meet on reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Stocks surge to all-time highs. Homebuilder sentiment drops to a seven-month low.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Friday, April 17th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: A ten-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon has officially taken hold, but the Lebanese army is already reporting Israeli shelling in the south. And as we speak, about forty countries are meeting to figure out how to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

ANDREW: Plus, homebuilder confidence just hit its lowest point since last September. We'll break down what that signals for the spring housing market.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at a record 7,041, up about a quarter of a percent. The Dow added 115 points to finish at 48,579. And the Nasdaq settled at 24,103, up about a third of a percent, notching its twelfth consecutive daily gain, the longest winning streak since July 2009. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.27 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is at 6.30 percent according to Freddie Mac. The rally continues to be driven by optimism that the Iran conflict may be winding down.

ANDREW: And that optimism has a lot of moving pieces behind it, so let's unpack the Middle East first. As we've been tracking all week, the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports is now in its fifth day. The Pentagon says thirteen ships have been turned away. About ten thousand service members, more than a hundred aircraft, and a dozen warships are enforcing it. The blockade is costing Iran an estimated four hundred million dollars a day in lost revenue, according to CNBC. But the bigger development this morning is the new Israel-Lebanon ceasefire that took effect overnight.

JENNY: Right, and this was Trump-brokered. A ten-day truce. But it sounds like it's already being tested?

ANDREW: It is. The Lebanese army confirmed overnight that Israeli forces shelled several villages in southern Lebanon, including Al-Khiam and Debbin. Al Jazeera also reported drone strikes in the Bekaa Valley and Israeli machine gun fire at an ambulance near the town of Kounin. That said, the ceasefire is largely holding in Beirut and northern Lebanon. Al-Monitor reports that displaced families are starting to trickle south, cautiously. Netanyahu said he agreed to the truce to advance peace efforts but Israeli troops are staying in what he calls a security zone inside Lebanese territory.

JENNY: And Trump invited both Netanyahu and Lebanese President Aoun to the White House?

ANDREW: He did. He described it as what could be the first meaningful talks between the two countries since 1983. Meanwhile, on the Iran front, Trump told Fox Business this week that the war is, quote, very close to over. He said another round of in-person negotiations could happen over the weekend in Pakistan. Uranium enrichment remains the key sticking point. And today, about thirty to forty countries led by French President Macron and British Prime Minister Starmer are meeting to discuss reopening the Strait of Hormuz once conditions allow. Germany, Italy, Canada, Australia, and the UAE are among them. The U.S. is notably absent. European countries including France, Belgium, and the Netherlands say they have mine-clearing capability ready to deploy.

JENNY: So the ceasefire deadline of April 21st, the Warsh confirmation hearing that same day, and potentially new Iran talks over the weekend. That's a lot converging in one window.

ANDREW: It really is. And on that Warsh hearing, which we've been following for weeks, the Senate Banking Committee has locked in next Tuesday, April 21st, at 10 a.m. His financial disclosures show assets between 131 and 209 million dollars, making him the wealthiest nominee ever for the Fed chair, according to CNBC. But Senator Thom Tillis is still threatening to block the final vote until a federal probe involving Chair Powell and the Fed is resolved. Republicans have a razor-thin majority on that committee, so they need his vote.

JENNY: And Powell has said he'll stay until a successor is confirmed. His term expires May 15th.

ANDREW: That's right. So the clock is ticking.

JENNY: Andrew, what's the picture looking like on the housing and mortgage side?

ANDREW: Not great, frankly. Let's start with homebuilder confidence. The NAHB index fell four points to 34 in April, the lowest reading since last September. That marks twenty-four consecutive months in negative territory. Every region declined. The component tracking prospective buyer traffic dropped to just 22, which is strikingly low for the start of the spring selling season.

JENNY: Is that all rate-driven, or is there something else at play?

ANDREW: It's a few things stacking up. Rates are part of it. The thirty-year fixed is at 6.30 percent, and the MBA has officially dropped its forecast for any Fed rate cuts this year. They now expect inflation closer to four percent by year-end, up from their original projection of 3.2 percent. That's largely because energy costs from the Iran conflict are flowing through to the broader economy. But builders are also dealing with higher material costs. According to the NAHB, sixty-two percent of builders say suppliers have raised prices because of fuel costs. Thirty-six percent of builders cut home prices in April with an average reduction of five percent.

ANDREW: And the existing home sales picture isn't any brighter. March sales fell 3.6 percent to an annualized rate of 3.98 million, according to the National Association of Realtors. That's the slowest March pace since 2009. The median sale price did tick up to about 409,000 dollars, the thirty-third straight month of year-over-year price gains. So prices are still rising but transactions are slowing. NAR chief economist Lawrence Yun pointed to lower consumer confidence and softer job growth holding buyers back.

JENNY: Prices up, sales down, confidence low. Not exactly the spring bounce the industry was hoping for.

ANDREW: No, and it underscores why there's so much riding on whether any Middle East resolution can bring energy prices down and give the Fed room to act.

ANDREW: Jenny, let's head to Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at 90 degrees and sunny again today. The drought across northeast Florida continues with no rain in sight.

JENNY: On the development front, a big announcement this week. The University of Florida has selected Edgemoor Infrastructure and Real Estate to lead the first phase of construction on the new UF Jacksonville graduate campus. We're talking about a 287-and-a-half-million-dollar project that will anchor the campus in the historic LaVilla neighborhood. The developer selection came after a competitive national process, and the team includes Clark Construction alongside several local firms.

ANDREW: That's a major investment. Any sense of the timeline?

JENNY: UF says initial programs will launch in existing facilities this year, with new construction to follow. It's shaping up to be one of the biggest higher education investments in Northeast Florida in years, and it should bring real momentum to the LaVilla neighborhood downtown.

JENNY: Updating a story we've been tracking, the JEA investigation continues. State Attorney Melissa Nelson's subpoena is demanding a broad set of communications involving CEO Vickie Cavey, City Council President Kevin Carrico, and Ballard Partners, the lobbying firm that employs former Mayor Lenny Curry. The subpoena covers records going back to last July. No indictments yet, but Mayor Deegan continues to characterize the Council's allegations as politically motivated. We'll keep watching.

JENNY: And a quick note on the Winn-Dixie headquarters deal we mentioned earlier this week. The twelve-million-dollar incentive package cleared the Mayor's Budget Review Committee and could be formally introduced to City Council on April 28th. That would retain five hundred existing jobs and add two hundred new ones, all at an average salary above a hundred thousand dollars, according to the Jax Daily Record.

ANDREW: Good for the Westside if it goes through.

JENNY: Agreed. Now, staying with me on the tech beat. Fortune published a striking report this week on AI adoption in the workplace, and the numbers tell a very different story than what most executives seem to believe. Roughly eighty percent of enterprise workers are either avoiding or outright rejecting the AI tools their companies are spending heavily to deploy.

ANDREW: Eighty percent? That's a staggering gap between executive ambition and what's actually happening on the ground.

JENNY: It really is. Fifty-four percent of workers told Fortune they bypassed their company's AI tools in the past month and did the work manually instead. Another third said they haven't touched AI at all. And here's the more striking finding. Twenty-nine percent of workers admit to actively sabotaging their company's AI rollout. Among Gen Z workers, that jumps to forty-four percent. We're talking about employees intentionally making AI look less effective or entering proprietary data into public tools to undermine the strategy.

ANDREW: What's driving the resistance?

JENNY: Trust, mostly. Only nine percent of workers say they trust AI for complex business decisions, compared to sixty-one percent of executives. That's a fifty-two-point gap. Nearly half of non-users say they simply prefer doing things the way they've always done them. About four in ten cite ethical concerns or data privacy worries. It's a real challenge for companies that have gone all-in on AI infrastructure but haven't brought their people along.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this weekend and into Tuesday. April 21st is shaping up as a pivotal day on multiple fronts. The Iran ceasefire expires that day. Kevin Warsh faces the Senate Banking Committee at 10 a.m. for his Fed chair confirmation hearing. And Trump has hinted that another round of U.S.-Iran talks could happen before then in Pakistan. Whether any of those dominoes fall could move markets, mortgage rates, and oil prices significantly heading into next week.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Friday. Have a great weekend.

ANDREW: We'll see you Monday.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Iran Blockade Tightens, Trump Threatens Powell, S and P Tops 7,000
item.description: The U.S. naval blockade of Iran enters day four as Trump brokers an Israel-Lebanon ceasefire. The president threatens to fire Fed Chair Powell. The S and P 500 closes above 7,000 for the first time.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Thursday, April 16th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: The U.S. naval blockade of Iran's ports is now fully operational, with thirteen ships turned away since Monday, and President Trump says the war is, quote, very close to over. Meanwhile, he's also picking a new fight closer to home, threatening to fire the Fed chair.

ANDREW: And the S and P 500 closed above seven thousand for the first time ever yesterday, erasing all of the losses from the start of the Iran war. A lot to get to.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at 7,022, up about eight tenths of a percent, a fresh all-time record. The Dow dipped slightly, closing at 48,464, down about a tenth of a percent. The Nasdaq rose to 24,016, up about one and a half percent, extending its winning streak to twelve consecutive sessions. That is the longest run for the Nasdaq since 2009. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.31 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at 6.30 percent according to Freddie Mac, down from 6.37 last week.

ANDREW: Let's start with the biggest story out of Washington, and honestly, there are several competing for that title today. The U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports is now in its fourth day, and the Pentagon says it is fully implemented. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said yesterday the blockade will continue, quote, as long as it takes, and that the U.S. remains locked and loaded to strike Iran's energy infrastructure if needed.

JENNY: How effective has the blockade actually been so far?

ANDREW: By the numbers, the Navy has turned away thirteen commercial vessels since Monday. On Tuesday, the first full day, only eight ships even attempted to transit the strait, most of them Iranian-flagged or under sanctions. For context, on a normal day, roughly sixty to seventy ships pass through Hormuz. So traffic has collapsed. The International Energy Agency's director, Fatih Birol, warned that Europe has about six weeks of jet fuel reserves left if this continues. Oil prices are up roughly forty percent since the war started in late February.

JENNY: And what about the diplomatic side? Trump said the war is close to ending.

ANDREW: He did, and there's some reason to take that seriously today. The president brokered a ten-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon, announced late yesterday. It took effect at five p.m. Eastern. Trump spoke directly with Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu and Lebanese President Aoun, and he's invited both to the White House for what he called the first meaningful talks between the two countries since 1983. That's a sidecar conflict, not the main Iran war, but it does signal that the administration is working multiple diplomatic channels at once. On the core Iran question, in-person talks through Pakistan could resume as early as this week. The ceasefire with Iran expires April 21st, so the clock is ticking.

JENNY: Speaking of ticking clocks, the Senate took another crack at the war powers question yesterday.

ANDREW: They did, and for the fourth time, it failed. The Senate voted 47 to 52 to reject a resolution from Senator Tammy Duckworth that would have forced the president to withdraw troops from Iran without congressional authorization. Every Republican except Rand Paul voted against it. Every Democrat except John Fetterman voted for it. The legal deadline under the War Powers Act is approaching fast. The administration notified Congress in early March, which means the sixty-day window likely expires around May 1st. At that point, Congress either authorizes the war, forces its end, or we enter genuinely uncharted constitutional territory.

JENNY: And on Tax Day, thousands of people marched in cities across the country, from New York to San Francisco, protesting the use of tax dollars to fund the Iran war and immigration enforcement. Organizers called it the largest Tax Day mobilization in years.

JENNY: And then there's the Fed situation. What happened there?

ANDREW: President Trump went on Fox Business yesterday and explicitly threatened to fire Jerome Powell if Powell doesn't leave when his term as chair expires next month. Powell has said he'll stay on as chair until a successor is confirmed. Trump's response was blunt. Quote, he's doing a bad job. He should be lowering interest rates. Now, legally, the president almost certainly cannot fire a Fed governor except for cause, which means serious misconduct. But the threat itself matters because it rattles markets and raises questions about central bank independence heading into a leadership transition. Kevin Warsh, Trump's nominee to replace Powell, has his Senate Banking Committee hearing set for Monday, April 21st. But Republican Senator Thom Tillis is still threatening to block the vote unless the Justice Department drops its investigation into a multibillion-dollar Fed headquarters renovation. Republicans have a razor-thin majority on that committee, so they need Tillis.

JENNY: So we could have no confirmed Fed chair when Powell's term ends in mid-May.

ANDREW: That is a real possibility, and it is exactly why Powell says he's staying until there's a successor. The uncertainty alone is worth watching.

JENNY: Andrew, what's happening on the mortgage side?

ANDREW: Some welcome news for buyers, actually. The thirty-year fixed rate dropped to 6.30 percent this week, according to Freddie Mac's weekly survey, down from 6.37 last week. That is a four-week low. Mortgage News Daily has it at 6.32 percent. Either way, we're meaningfully below where we were a year ago, when rates were closer to 6.83 percent.

JENNY: Is that the Iran situation pulling rates down? That seems counterintuitive.

ANDREW: It's a mix. The blockade and war fears are pushing investors into safer assets like Treasuries, which pushes yields down, which pushes mortgage rates down. But at the same time, the PPI report from Monday showed energy-driven inflation running hot at four percent year over year. So you've got this tug of war. Rates could snap back quickly if the war ends and money flows out of bonds. Or they could drop further if the conflict escalates and the economy slows. For anyone shopping for a home right now, the window of relatively lower rates is real, but it may not last. The Fed is holding steady at three and a half to three and three-quarter percent on the funds rate, and Chair Powell, while he's still in the job, has signaled patience. The next real data point is the April jobs report in early May.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high of 88 degrees today, partly cloudy, with southeast winds around ten to fifteen miles per hour. No rain in the forecast, and the drought continues.

JENNY: On the development front, a big milestone for downtown Jacksonville. The city issued a building permit this week for Gateway Jax's Block N8, a twenty-two-story mixed-use tower in the Pearl Square development. The project cost is just under 145 million dollars.

ANDREW: What's going in the building?

JENNY: It's a mix. 537 rental units, including 394 apartments and 143 short-term rental units, plus retail space, a restaurant, a gym, and parking. The contractor is PCL Construction. And remember, city council already approved a combined 59 million dollars in incentive grants for the broader Gateway Jax project. According to the Jax Daily Record, this is one of the largest single building permits in downtown Jacksonville's history.

ANDREW: And this is the same Pearl Square development that's been in the works for a while now?

JENNY: Exactly. Gateway Jax has been moving through phases, and this is the biggest piece yet. The site is at Beaver and Pearl streets, and it's part of a broader push to add density and street-level activity to what's been a pretty underused part of downtown.

JENNY: Updating a story we've been tracking, the JEA investigation. No new subpoenas or indictments to report since we last covered this, but the situation remains active. State Attorney Melissa Nelson's office subpoenaed JEA earlier this month for a broad set of communications involving CEO Vickie Cavey, Council President Kevin Carrico, and Ballard Partners, which employs former Mayor Lenny Curry. The subpoena covers records going back to July 2025 and includes keyword searches for terms like racist and toxic. Mayor Deegan continues to characterize the allegations against Cavey as a smear campaign. We'll keep watching for any developments on that front.

JENNY: And one more Jacksonville note. The Winn-Dixie headquarters incentive deal we mentioned earlier this week cleared the Mayor's Budget Review Committee and is headed to city council. The package is worth about 12 million dollars in grants and tax rebates, tied to 200 new jobs at an average salary above 100 thousand dollars. That vote could come as early as April 28th.

JENNY: Andrew, let me bring you back in for the tech beat. Nvidia made a pretty interesting move this week.

ANDREW: What did they do?

JENNY: They released what they're calling Ising, the first family of open-source AI models designed specifically for quantum computing. There are two main models. One is a 35-billion-parameter model that can calibrate quantum processors, cutting calibration time from days to hours. The other handles quantum error correction and is reportedly two and a half times faster and three times more accurate than existing tools. Major research labs are already adopting it, including Fermilab, Harvard, and Lawrence Berkeley National Lab. The weights are available on Hugging Face under an Apache 2.0 license.

ANDREW: So Nvidia is basically trying to own the software layer for quantum hardware, the same way they own the GPU software stack for AI training.

JENNY: That's exactly the play. And it's open source, which means they're betting on ecosystem lock-in rather than licensing revenue. If every quantum lab standardizes on Nvidia's tools, it doesn't matter who builds the hardware.

JENNY: In other tech news, AMD surged nearly eight percent yesterday on continued AI demand momentum. The company reports earnings May 5th, and investors are optimistic after a strong quarter. AMD's data center segment hit a record 5.4 billion dollars in Q4, up 39 percent year over year, and the stock has roughly tripled in the past twelve months.

ANDREW: That Gartner survey that came out today is worth flagging too.

JENNY: Right. Gartner surveyed nearly 800 IT leaders and found that only 28 percent of enterprise AI projects fully meet their ROI expectations, while 20 percent fail outright. So even as the semiconductor stocks are surging, the companies actually deploying AI are still struggling to turn pilot projects into real business value. It's a useful reality check.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this Monday. Kevin Warsh sits down before the Senate Banking Committee at 10 a.m. for his Fed chair confirmation hearing. This is the first time the public will hear Warsh lay out his views on interest rates, inflation, and central bank independence since being nominated in January. Watch for two things. First, how he handles questions about Trump's pressure to cut rates. And second, whether Thom Tillis shows up ready to vote or still dug in on his demand to kill the DOJ investigation. If Tillis holds firm, Warsh's path to confirmation gets very narrow, and the Fed leadership vacuum extends deeper into May.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Thursday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Iran Blockade Day Two, PPI Surprises, Anthropic Passes OpenAI
item.description: Day two of the US naval blockade of Iran as oil drops four percent on talk of renewed negotiations. Wholesale inflation hits a three-year high but comes in lighter than feared. And Anthropic's revenue surge is rattling OpenAI's investors.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Wednesday, April fifteenth, twenty twenty-six. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: We are now forty-eight hours into the first American naval blockade since the Cuban Missile Crisis, and something unexpected happened yesterday. Oil prices dropped.

ANDREW: And in the AI world, a revenue milestone that has some of the biggest names in venture capital rethinking where they've placed their bets.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at sixty-nine sixty-seven, up one point two percent. The Dow added three hundred eighteen points to close at forty-eight thousand five thirty-six, up about two thirds of a percent. The Nasdaq led the day at twenty-three thousand six thirty-nine, up nearly two percent, marking its tenth consecutive day of gains. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at four point two nine percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at roughly six point four percent according to Bankrate. A strong session across the board, driven in part by an inflation number that wasn't as bad as Wall Street feared.

ANDREW: Let's start there. The Bureau of Labor Statistics released March wholesale inflation data yesterday, and the headline was ugly at first glance. The Producer Price Index hit four percent year over year, a three-year high. But the details told a different story.

JENNY: How so? Because four percent wholesale inflation sounds like it's moving in the wrong direction.

ANDREW: It is moving in the wrong direction, but not as fast as economists predicted. Wall Street expected the monthly number to jump one point one percent because of the Iran-driven oil shock. Instead it came in at zero point five percent. And when you strip out food and energy, core PPI rose just one tenth of a percent for the month. That tells you the inflationary pressure is almost entirely energy, not broad-based. Markets loved it.

JENNY: And that helps explain why stocks rallied even with a war going on in the Middle East.

ANDREW: Exactly. Now, updating the story we have been tracking on the Iran blockade, we are now in day two of the US naval blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz. The Pentagon says no commercial ships got past American forces in the first twenty-four hours. Six merchant vessels were turned away, including a Chinese tanker that aborted its crossing.

JENNY: And yet oil dropped four percent yesterday. That seems counterintuitive with a blockade tightening.

ANDREW: It does. Brent crude settled around ninety-five dollars a barrel, down from above a hundred. The reason is diplomatic signals. Bloomberg and CNBC both reported that the White House is actively working to arrange a second round of peace talks with Iran. Trump told the New York Post that, quote, something could be happening over the next two days in Pakistan. Markets are reading that as a de-escalation signal, even while the Navy is actively blocking ships. It is worth noting that the international response has been sharply critical. China called the blockade contrary to the international community's interests, and the EU, the UK, and Australia have all publicly withheld support.

JENNY: It is worth remembering that the ceasefire both sides agreed to on April eighth expires on April twenty-first. So there is a hard deadline approaching.

ANDREW: There is. And there is one more national story before we move on. Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott announced yesterday that Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing for Federal Reserve Chair has been scheduled for ten a.m. on April twenty-first. Warsh filed his sixty-nine page financial disclosure on Monday, and it reveals substantial wealth, including extensive holdings tied to his wife Jane Lauder, the granddaughter of cosmetics mogul Estee Lauder.

JENNY: Is the Tillis problem resolved?

ANDREW: It is not. Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina is still threatening to withhold his vote unless the Justice Department drops its investigation into current Fed Chair Jay Powell over congressional testimony related to a multibillion-dollar Fed headquarters renovation. Republicans have a razor-thin majority on the committee, so they cannot afford to lose Tillis. Powell's term expires May fifteenth, and the Fed has said he would stay on as acting chair if no successor is confirmed. Bond markets are pricing in uncertainty around the transition, and that is one more reason the ten-year yield has been so volatile lately.

JENNY: So April twenty-first is shaping up to be a big day on multiple fronts. Andrew, let me take us down to Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at eighty-four degrees and abundant sunshine today. Winds out of the south-southwest shifting to the east-southeast at ten to fifteen miles per hour. The drought continues with no rain in the forecast.

JENNY: Now, updating the JEA investigation we have been following. On Friday, State Attorney Melissa Nelson issued a subpoena to JEA demanding a broad set of communication records. According to the Florida Trib and News4Jax, the subpoena targets emails and texts between board members about CEO Vickie Cavey, discussions involving City Council President Kevin Carrico, and any communications to or from Ballard Partners, the lobbying firm that employs former Mayor Lenny Curry.

ANDREW: That is a wide net. What specifically are prosecutors looking for?

JENNY: The subpoena asks for any records containing a mix of key words including the words racist and toxic, which gives you a sense of the workplace culture allegations at the center of this. The City Council's investigative committee has now met three times. Mayor Deegan continues to call the whole thing a smear campaign, arguing it started because Cavey canceled a lobbying contract with Ballard Partners. But the fact that the State Attorney's office is now issuing subpoenas means this has moved beyond internal politics.

ANDREW: Is there any timeline on where this goes next?

JENNY: Not yet. No indictments, no formal charges. But the subpoena suggests the investigation is expanding, not winding down.

JENNY: On a brighter note for Jacksonville, the twelve-million-dollar incentive package to keep Winn-Dixie's headquarters on the Westside cleared the Mayor's Budget Review Committee on Sunday. According to the Jax Daily Record, the deal includes a six point five million dollar retention grant and five point five million in tax rebates. In exchange, Winn-Dixie would invest at least sixty-five million dollars, including forty-eight million in store renovations and seventeen million at headquarters, and add two hundred jobs at an average salary of one hundred thousand dollars a year while retaining five hundred existing positions.

ANDREW: When does the full Council vote?

JENNY: The legislation could be introduced on April twenty-eighth. If it passes, the retention grants would start flowing in October twenty twenty-seven. For context, this is the biggest corporate retention deal Jacksonville has pursued since the CSX headquarters relocation, and with Aldi now owning the parent company, there has been real anxiety about whether the grocer would eventually consolidate operations out of state.

JENNY: One more quick Jacksonville note. We have been tracking the JTA fare reduction pilot that started in February, cutting bus fares from a dollar seventy-five to a dollar. Ridership fell eighteen percent in February and ten percent in March compared to last year, which was the opposite of what the pilot intended. The JTA board is expected to take up the issue later this month, and the question now is whether they extend the pilot, adjust it, or pull the plug entirely.

JENNY: Andrew, what is happening on the mortgage side?

ANDREW: The home lending picture right now is all about volatility. The thirty-year fixed rate is sitting around six point four percent according to Bankrate, essentially flat from last week. But flat is misleading. The Mortgage Rate Variability Index jumped to seven out of ten last week, up from five the week before, meaning the spread between what different lenders are offering has widened significantly. That is a direct consequence of the Iran situation making bond markets unpredictable.

JENNY: Are buyers pulling back?

ANDREW: They are. The MBA reported that mortgage applications fell eight tenths of a percent for the week ending April third. Purchase applications ticked up one percent on the week, which sounds okay, but on a year-over-year basis purchase apps were down seven percent. That is the first annual decline since January twenty twenty-five. Refinance activity dropped three percent and is at its lowest level since December. Borrowers are essentially frozen, waiting to see if rates come down, and lenders are widening their margins because they cannot predict where the ten-year Treasury is headed next.

JENNY: So for someone trying to buy a house right now, what is the practical advice?

ANDREW: Lock early and shop aggressively. When the variability index is this high, the difference between lenders can be a quarter point or more. That matters a lot on a thirty-year loan. And keep watching that ten-year yield. If the Iran situation de-escalates, Treasury yields could drop, and mortgage rates would follow within days.

JENNY: Speaking of things moving fast, let me take us to the AI beat, because the numbers coming out of Anthropic this week are turning heads.

JENNY: Multiple outlets are now reporting that Anthropic has hit thirty billion dollars in annualized revenue, up from nine billion at the end of last year and nineteen billion just last month. That puts it ahead of OpenAI, which is at roughly twenty-five billion. The growth has been staggering. TechCrunch reported yesterday that some investors who have backed both companies are now questioning OpenAI's eight hundred fifty-two billion dollar valuation.

ANDREW: How is OpenAI responding to that?

JENNY: With a memo. The Verge obtained an internal memo from OpenAI's chief revenue officer Denise Dresser that pushes back hard on Anthropic's stated numbers. The memo claims Anthropic overstates its run rate by roughly eight billion dollars through, quote, accounting treatment that makes revenue look bigger than it is, including grossing up revenue share arrangements with Amazon and Google. OpenAI is essentially arguing that the real gap is much smaller than the headlines suggest.

ANDREW: Is there merit to that claim?

JENNY: It is hard to know without seeing both companies' books, and neither is public yet. But here is what we do know. Anthropic says eighty percent of its revenue comes from business customers, compared to OpenAI's more consumer-heavy mix. And Anthropic's coding tools, particularly Claude Code, are reportedly generating two and a half billion dollars a year on their own. Even if you discount the headline number by eight billion, Anthropic's growth rate is extraordinary, and the secondary market agrees. Demand for Anthropic shares has been, in the words of one investor who spoke to the Financial Times, nearly insatiable.

ANDREW: Meanwhile, on the product side, OpenAI rolled out what it is calling GPT five point four Cyber, a cybersecurity-specific model. That is a direct response to Anthropic's Mythos model, which has been making waves for its ability to find software vulnerabilities faster than traditional methods. Both companies are clearly racing to own the enterprise security market.

JENNY: The AI arms race is very much a revenue race now, not just a benchmarks race. And for enterprise buyers, that competition is good news. It means more specialized models, more aggressive pricing, and more pressure on both companies to prove their tools actually deliver return on investment.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch. April twenty-first is shaping up as a pivotal day. That morning, Kevin Warsh sits before the Senate Banking Committee for his Fed Chair confirmation hearing, the most consequential central banking appointment in years. And that same day, the US-Iran ceasefire expires. If talks resume before then, markets could rally further. If they do not, expect oil back above a hundred dollars and mortgage rates to climb. Circle Monday on your calendar.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Wednesday. Have a great rest of your day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — US Navy Blockade of Iran Enters Day One, Inflation Spikes to 3.3 Percent
item.description: The US naval blockade of Iranian ports enters its first full day as oil tops 104 dollars a barrel. March inflation surged to 3.3 percent on war-driven energy costs. Plus, Anthropic passes OpenAI in revenue and Jacksonville weighs a 12 million dollar deal to keep Winn-Dixie.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Tuesday, April fourteenth, twenty twenty-six. This is The Morning Brief. I am Jenny.

ANDREW: And I am Andrew.

JENNY: The United States Navy is now enforcing a full blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz, and oil prices are back above a hundred dollars a barrel. We have the latest on what that means for markets, inflation, and the talks that might still pull both sides back from the brink.

ANDREW: And in a milestone that would have seemed impossible a year ago, Anthropic has officially passed OpenAI in revenue. We will get into the numbers and what is driving that surge.

JENNY: Let us get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed yesterday at sixty-eight eighty-six, up about one percent. The Dow added three hundred two points, closing at forty-eight thousand two eighteen, up six tenths of a percent. The Nasdaq led the way, up one point two percent to twenty-three thousand one eighty-four. That was the highest close for the S and P since before the Iran war began on February twenty-eighth, a remarkable round trip. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at four point two nine percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at six point three one percent according to Mortgage News Daily, the lowest in about four weeks.

ANDREW: Let us start with what is dominating everything right now. The US naval blockade of Iranian ports officially began yesterday morning at ten a.m. Eastern, after weekend peace talks in Pakistan collapsed. More than ten thousand sailors, marines, and airmen, along with over a dozen warships, are now enforcing a no-entry zone around all Iranian ports on the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman. In the first twenty-four hours, no ships made it through. Six merchant vessels were directed to turn around.

JENNY: And this is different from what Iran had already been doing with the strait, right?

ANDREW: It is. Iran had been blocking and taxing traffic through Hormuz since the war began on February twenty-eighth, charging tolls north of a million dollars per ship. The ceasefire brokered in Pakistan on April eighth was supposed to reopen the strait, but Iran never followed through. Abu Dhabi's national oil company confirmed ships were still being turned away. So the US effectively flipped the script. Central Command clarified that non-Iranian port traffic can still transit the strait freely, but nothing goes in or out of Iranian waters.

JENNY: What is this doing to oil prices?

ANDREW: Brent crude jumped to about a hundred and two dollars a barrel. US crude topped a hundred and four. And that brings us to what the war is already doing to prices here at home. The March consumer price index came out last Thursday, and it was the hottest print in years. Headline inflation surged to three point three percent year over year, up from two point four in February. The Bureau of Labor Statistics said energy prices rose nearly eleven percent in March. Gasoline alone was up twenty-one percent, and that accounted for roughly three quarters of the entire monthly increase.

JENNY: So the war is now showing up directly in consumer prices.

ANDREW: In a big way. And with oil still above a hundred dollars, the April report is unlikely to be much better. That is the backdrop for one of the most consequential Fed leadership transitions in modern history. Jay Powell's term as chair ends May fifteenth. President Trump's nominee, Kevin Warsh, was named back on January thirtieth, but his confirmation has been stalled. The good news is that Bloomberg and Axios both reported today that the Senate Banking Committee has now set Warsh's confirmation hearing for next Monday, April twenty-first.

JENNY: So there is a timeline now.

ANDREW: There is, but there is also a significant obstacle. Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina says he will not vote for Warsh until the Justice Department drops its investigation into Powell. A Tillis spokesperson softened that slightly, saying the senator will support Warsh once the DOJ investigation concludes, but there is no timeline on that. New financial disclosures filed ahead of the hearing show Warsh's net worth is in the nine figures, which would make him the wealthiest Fed chair on record. Powell has said he will stay on as acting chair if his replacement is not confirmed by May fifteenth.

JENNY: So we could have an acting Fed chair right as inflation is running at three point three percent.

ANDREW: That is the scenario the bond market is watching closely. Jenny, over to you on the home front.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at highs in the low eighties and dry conditions today. And dry is the word to focus on. The city is in the middle of a severe drought with a rainfall deficit in the twenty to twenty-five inch range. Meteorologists are giving us only about a ten percent chance of rain this week, so water conservation remains a concern.

JENNY: Updating a story we have been tracking closely. State Attorney Melissa Nelson's office sent JEA a subpoena on Friday, and the scope is significant. She is asking for emails, texts, and communication records involving CEO Vickie Cavey, City Council President Kevin Carrico, and the lobbying firm Ballard Partners, which employs former Mayor Lenny Curry. The subpoena also asks for records containing specific keywords including racist and toxic.

ANDREW: What exactly is the State Attorney investigating?

JENNY: It connects to a JEA board appointment controversy. Carrico sent a text saying he owed a, quote, big favor to a friend and planned to put that person on the JEA board. That surfaced publicly, and the situation escalated. The City Council formed a special investigative committee that has now met three times. Mayor Donna Deegan has called the allegations against Cavey a smear campaign, arguing this all started because Cavey refused to renew a lobbying contract with Ballard Partners. So the subpoena is trying to sort out whether the pressure on Cavey involves legitimate workplace concerns or political retaliation. Two JEA board positions are currently on hold during the investigation. If you lived through the twenty-nineteen JEA privatization scandal, this dynamic is going to feel familiar.

JENNY: Also in Jacksonville, a twelve million dollar incentive package to keep Winn-Dixie's headquarters in the city cleared the Mayor's Budget Review Committee yesterday. The deal has two parts. A six and a half million dollar headquarters retention grant paid over five years, and five and a half million in property tax rebates over twenty years. In return, Winn-Dixie is committing to a sixty-five million dollar capital investment and two hundred new jobs paying at least a hundred thousand dollars a year at its Edgewood Court offices. That would bring the headquarters workforce to seven hundred.

ANDREW: Two hundred jobs at six figures is meaningful. What is the timeline?

JENNY: The legislation could be introduced to City Council on April twenty-eighth. The concern if this falls through is that Winn-Dixie's parent company could consolidate headquarters operations elsewhere. Jacksonville has been their home base for decades, so both sides have motivation to close this.

JENNY: One more quick local item. The Jacksonville Transportation Authority's fare reduction pilot is producing the opposite of what was intended. The Jax Daily Record reported that ridership on fixed bus routes fell about eighteen percent in February and ten percent in March compared to last year, even after JTA slashed fares from a dollar seventy-five to a dollar. The agency expected cheaper fares would bring more riders. The early data suggests the problem may not be price but network design and service frequency. Andrew, what are you seeing on the mortgage side?

ANDREW: The thirty-year fixed at six point three one percent per Mortgage News Daily is the lowest reading in about four weeks. That reflects some easing tied to ceasefire hopes and the rally in Treasuries. But here is the tension. The March CPI we just discussed, three point three percent headline, is working directly against any sustained rate relief. Lenders price in inflation expectations, and when you see energy-driven spikes like this, the market gets cautious about where rates are headed.

JENNY: So rates came down slightly, but the inflation data could push them right back up.

ANDREW: That is exactly the risk. The ten-year Treasury at four point two nine is holding relatively steady, but if oil stays above a hundred dollars and the April CPI shows continued energy pass-through, mortgage rates could drift back toward the high sixes. For buyers, this narrow window in the low sixes may look good in hindsight. On the regulatory front, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Federal Housing Finance Agency were both expected to issue guidance this month on artificial intelligence use in underwriting. That has now slipped to May at the earliest, according to National Mortgage News. For lenders holding back automated decisioning investments waiting for clarity, that is another month in limbo.

JENNY: Speaking of AI, the tech beat had a massive week. Anthropic has officially passed OpenAI in revenue. The company hit thirty billion dollars in annualized revenue, up from about fourteen billion in February. That is more than a doubling in roughly eight weeks. And more than a thousand business customers are now spending over a million dollars a year with Anthropic.

ANDREW: What is driving growth that fast?

JENNY: Claude Code is the biggest single factor. It is generating over two and a half billion in annual revenue on its own and accounts for more than half of all enterprise spending on Anthropic products. But eighty percent of Anthropic's total revenue comes from enterprise API usage, not consumer subscriptions. They have deep traction in financial services, legal, and now healthcare after acquiring a biotech startup called Coefficient Bio earlier this month to push into drug discovery.

JENNY: Meta also made a notable move last week. They launched a new model called Muse Spark, the first from their Superintelligence Labs division under Alexandr Wang. And here is the key detail. It is proprietary. The weights are closed. That is a sharp departure from Meta's open-source Llama strategy that defined them for the past two years.

ANDREW: So Meta is going closed-source.

JENNY: For this model, at least. They said they hope to open-source future versions, but Muse Spark is powering the Meta AI app right now as a closed system. Meta said the model matches their older mid-size Llama for an order of magnitude less compute. It is a signal that even the loudest open-source champion in AI will keep its best work proprietary when the competitive pressure is high enough.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week. Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing next Monday, April twenty-first, before the Senate Banking Committee. This is the first public vetting of the person who could be running the Federal Reserve as soon as mid-May. Inflation is at three point three percent. The Iran war is keeping oil above a hundred dollars. And the nominee's financial disclosures reveal potential conflicts that senators from both parties will want to probe. Watch whether Tillis signals flexibility on his hold, and listen closely for any hints from Warsh on the rate path. The bond market and mortgage rates will move on what he says.

JENNY: That is your Morning Brief for Tuesday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We will see you tomorrow.
